content:
# Genesis Chapter 29 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 16r–16v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_029_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_029_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 25–26 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0029 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0030

---

*Jacob Arrives at the Well in the East (vv. 1–8)*

**29:1** And Jacob lifted his feet [וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו] and went [וַיֵּלֶךְ] to the land of [אֶרֶץ] the sons of [בְּנֵי] the East [קֶדֶם].

**29:2** And he saw [וַיַּרְא] and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — a well [בְּאֵר] in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] there [שָׁם] three flocks [שְׁלֹשָׁה עֶדְרֵי-צֹאן] of sheep lying down [רֹבְצִים] beside it [עָלֶיהָ] — for from that [הַ] well [בְּאֵר הַהִוא] they watered [יַשְׁקוּ] the flocks [הָעֲדָרִים] — and the stone [וְהָאֶבֶן] was great [גְדֹלָה] upon [עַל] the mouth of [פִּי] the well [הַבְּאֵר].

**29:3** And all the flocks [וְנֶאֶסְפוּ-שָׁמָּה כָל-הָעֲדָרִים] would gather there [שָׁמָּה] and they would roll away [וְגָלֲלוּ] the stone [אֶת-הָאֶבֶן] from [מֵעַל] the mouth of [פִּי] the well [הַבְּאֵר] and water [וְהִשְׁקוּ] the sheep [אֶת-הַצֹּאן] and they would return [וְהֵשִׁיבוּ] the stone [אֶת-הָאֶבֶן] upon [עַל-פִּי] the mouth of [פִּי] the well [הַבְּאֵר] to its place [לִמְקֹמָהּ].

**29:4** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם יַעֲקֹב] to them: My brothers [אַחַי] — from where are you [מֵאַיִן אַתֶּם]? And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: From Haran [מֵחָרָן] we are [אֲנַחְנוּ].

**29:5** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to them: Do you know [הַיְדַעְתֶּם] Laban [אֶת-לָבָן] son of [בֶּן] Nahor [נָחוֹר]? And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: We know [יָדָעְנוּ].

**29:6** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to them: Is there peace [הֲשָׁלוֹם לוֹ] to him? And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Peace [שָׁלוֹם] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — Rachel [רָחֵל] his daughter [בִּתּוֹ] is coming [בָּאָה] with the sheep [עִם-הַצֹּאן].

**29:7** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Behold [הֵן] — the day [הַיּוֹם] is still great [עוֹד גָּדוֹל] — it is not [לֹא] time [עֵת] to gather [הֵאָסֵף] the livestock [הַמִּקְנֶה] — water [הַשְׁקוּ] the sheep [הַצֹּאן] and go [וּלְכוּ] and pasture [רְעוּ].

**29:8** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: We cannot [לֹא נוּכַל] until [עַד] all the flocks [אֲשֶׁר יֵאָסְפוּ כָּל-הָעֲדָרִים] are gathered [יֵאָסְפוּ] and they roll away [וְגָלֲלוּ] the stone [אֶת-הָאֶבֶן] from [מֵעַל] the mouth of [פִּי] the well [הַבְּאֵר] — and then we will water [וְהִשְׁקִינוּ] the sheep [הַצֹּאן].

---

*Jacob Meets Rachel — Kisses Her and Weeps (vv. 9–14)*

**29:9** While he was still [עוֹד] speaking [מְדַבֵּר] with them [עִמָּם] — and Rachel [וְרָחֵל] had come [בָּאָה] with the sheep [עִם-הַצֹּאן] that were [אֲשֶׁר] her father's [לְאָבִיהָ] — for a [כִּי] shepherdess [רֹעָה] she was [הִיא].

**29:10** And it was [וַיְהִי] — as soon as [כַּאֲשֶׁר] Jacob saw [רָאָה יַעֲקֹב] Rachel [אֶת-רָחֵל] daughter of [בַּת] Laban [לָבָן] his mother's [אִמּוֹ] brother [אֲחִי] — and the sheep of [אֶת-צֹאן] Laban [לָבָן] his mother's [אִמּוֹ] brother [אֲחִי] — that Jacob drew near [וַיִּגַּשׁ יַעֲקֹב] and rolled away [וַיָּגֶל] the stone [אֶת-הָאֶבֶן] from [מֵעַל] the mouth of [פִּי] the well [הַבְּאֵר] and watered [וַיַּשְׁקְ] the sheep of [אֶת-צֹאן] Laban [לָבָן] his mother's [אִמּוֹ] brother [אֲחִי].

**29:11** And Jacob kissed [וַיִּשַּׁק יַעֲקֹב] Rachel [לְרָחֵל] and he lifted up [וַיִּשָּׂא] his voice [אֶת-קֹלוֹ] and wept [וַיֵּבְךְּ].

**29:12** And Jacob told [וַיַּגֵּד יַעֲקֹב] Rachel [לְרָחֵל] that [כִּי] he was [הוּא] a kinsman [אֲחִי] of her father [אָבִיהָ] — and that [וְכִי] he was [הוּא] the son of [בֶּן] Rebekah [רִבְקָה] — and she ran [וַתָּרָץ] and told [וַתַּגֵּד] her father [לְאָבִיהָ].

**29:13** And it was [וַיְהִי] — as soon as [כִּשְׁמֹעַ] Laban heard [לָבָן] the report of [אֶת-שֵׁמַע] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] son of [בֶּן] his sister [אֲחֹתוֹ] — that he ran [וַיָּרָץ] to meet him [לִקְרָאתוֹ] and embraced him [וַיְחַבֶּק-לוֹ] and kissed him [וַיְנַשֶּׁק-לוֹ] and brought him [וַיְבִיאֵהוּ] to his house [אֶל-בֵּיתוֹ] — and he told [וַיְסַפֵּר] to Laban [לְלָבָן] all [אֶת] these [הָאֵלֶּה] things [כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים].

**29:14** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ לָבָן] to him: Surely [אַךְ] you are [עַצְמִי] my bone [עַצְמִי] and my flesh [וּבְשָׂרִי] — and he stayed [וַיֵּשֶׁב] with him [עִמּוֹ] a month [חֹדֶשׁ] of days [יָמִים].

---

*Jacob Agrees to Work for Rachel (vv. 15–20)*

**29:15** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב]: Because [הֲכִי] you are [אַחִי] my brother [אַחִי] — should you serve me [וַעֲבַדְתַּנִי] for nothing [חִנָּם]? Tell me [הַגִּידָה לִּי] — what shall be your wages [מַה-מַּשְׂכֻּרְתֶּךָ]?

**29:16** Now Laban had [וּלְלָבָן] two daughters [שְׁתֵּי בָנוֹת] — the name of [שֵׁם] the greater [הַגְּדֹלָה] was Leah [לֵאָה] and the name of [וְשֵׁם] the younger [הַקְּטַנָּה] was Rachel [רָחֵל].

**29:17** And Leah's [לֵאָה] eyes [עֵינֵי] were weak [רַכּוֹת] — but Rachel [וְרָחֵל] was beautiful of form [יְפַת-תֹּאַר] and beautiful of appearance [וִיפַת מַרְאֶה].

**29:18** And Jacob loved [וַיֶּאֱהַב יַעֲקֹב] Rachel [אֶת-רָחֵל] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will serve you [אֶעֱבָדְךָ] seven years [שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים] for Rachel [בְּרָחֵל] your daughter [בִּתְּךָ] the younger [הַקְּטַנָּה].

**29:19** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן]: Better [טוֹב] my giving her [תִּתִּי אֹתָהּ] to you [לְךָ] than my giving her [מִתִּתִּי אֹתָהּ] to another man [לְאִישׁ אַחֵר] — stay [שְׁבָה] with me [עִמָּדִי].

**29:20** And Jacob served [וַיַּעֲבֹד יַעֲקֹב] for Rachel [בְּרָחֵל] seven years [שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים] — and they were [וַיִּהְיוּ] in his eyes [בְּעֵינָיו] as a few [כְּיָמִים] days [אֲחָדִים] in his loving her [בְּאַהֲבָתוֹ אֹתָהּ].

---

*The Wedding Night — Laban Deceives Jacob with Leah (vv. 21–25)*

**29:21** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to Laban [אֶל-לָבָן]: Give [הָבָה] my wife [אֶת-אִשְׁתִּי] — for [כִּי] my days [יָמַי] are fulfilled [מָלְאוּ] — and let me come in [וְאָבוֹאָה] to her [אֵלֶיהָ].

**29:22** And Laban gathered [וַיֶּאֱסֹף לָבָן] all the men of [אֶת-כָּל-אַנְשֵׁי] the place [הַמָּקוֹם] and made a feast [וַיַּעַשׂ מִשְׁתֶּה].

**29:23** And it was [וַיְהִי] in the evening [בָּעֶרֶב] — that he took [וַיִּקַּח] Leah [אֶת-לֵאָה] his daughter [בִּתּוֹ] and brought her [וַיָּבֵא אֹתָהּ] to him [אֵלָיו] — and he went in [וַיָּבֹא] to her [אֵלֶיהָ].

**29:24** And Laban gave [וַיִּתֵּן לָבָן] to her [לָהּ] Zilpah [אֶת-זִלְפָּה] his maidservant [שִׁפְחָתוֹ] to Leah [לְלֵאָה] his daughter [בִּתּוֹ] as a maidservant [לְשִׁפְחָה].

**29:25** And it was [וַיְהִי] in the morning [בַּבֹּקֶר] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] — she was Leah [הִוא לֵאָה]! And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Laban [אֶל-לָבָן]: What is this [מַה-זֹּאת] you have done [עָשִׂיתָ] to me [לִי]? Was it not [הֲלֹא] for Rachel [בְּרָחֵל] that I served [עָבַדְתִּי] with you [עִמָּךְ]? And why [וְלָמָּה] have you deceived me [רִמִּיתָנִי]?

---

*Laban Justifies Himself — Jacob Marries Rachel Too (vv. 26–30)*

**29:26** And Laban said [וַיֹּאמֶר לָבָן]: It is not done so [לֹא-יֵעָשֶׂה כֵן] in our place [בִּמְקוֹמֵנוּ] — to give [לָתֵת] the younger [הַצְּעִירָה] before [לִפְנֵי] the firstborn [הַבְּכִירָה].

**29:27** Fulfill [מַלֵּא] the week of [שְׁבֻעַ] this one [זֹאת] — and we will give [וְנִתְּנָה] you also this one [לְךָ גַם-אֶת-זֹאת] in exchange for [בַּעֲבֹדָה] the service [בַּעֲבֹדָה] that you will serve [אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲבֹד] with me [עִמָּדִי] yet [עוֹד] another seven [שֶׁבַע] years [שָׁנִים] more [אֲחֵרוֹת].

**29:28** And Jacob did so [וַיַּעַשׂ יַעֲקֹב כֵּן] and fulfilled [וַיְמַלֵּא] the week of [שְׁבֻעַ] this one [זֹאת] — and he gave [וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ] him [לוֹ] Rachel [אֶת-רָחֵל] his daughter [בִּתּוֹ] to him as a wife [לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה].

**29:29** And Laban gave [וַיִּתֵּן לָבָן] to Rachel [לְרָחֵל] his daughter [בִּתּוֹ] Bilhah [אֶת-בִּלְהָה] his maidservant [שִׁפְחָתוֹ] to her [לָהּ] as a maidservant [לְשִׁפְחָה].

**29:30** And he went in [וַיָּבֹא] also [גַּם] to Rachel [אֶל-רָחֵל] and he also loved [וַיֶּאֱהַב גַּם] Rachel [אֶת-רָחֵל] more than [מִ] Leah [לֵאָה] — and he served [וַיַּעֲבֹד] with him [עִמּוֹ] yet [עוֹד] seven years [שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים] more [אֲחֵרוֹת].

---

*The LORD Opens Leah's Womb — Birth of Four Sons (vv. 31–35)*

**29:31** And the LORD saw [וַיַּרְא יְהוָה] that [כִּי] Leah was hated [שְׂנוּאָה לֵאָה] — and he opened [וַיִּפְתַּח] her womb [אֶת-רַחְמָהּ] — but Rachel [וְרָחֵל] was barren [עֲקָרָה].

**29:32** And Leah conceived [וַתַּהַר לֵאָה] and bore [וַתֵּלֶד] a son [בֵּן] and called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Reuben [רְאוּבֵן] — for she said [כִּי אָמְרָה]: For [כִּי] the LORD has looked upon [רָאָה יְהוָה] my affliction [בְּעָנְיִי] — for now [כִּי עַתָּה] my husband [יֶאֱהָבַנִי אִישִׁי] will love me [יֶאֱהָבַנִי].

**29:33** And she conceived again [וַתַּהַר עוֹד] and bore [וַתֵּלֶד] a son [בֵּן] and said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Because [כִּי] the LORD has heard [שָׁמַע יְהוָה] that [כִּי] I am hated [שְׂנוּאָה אָנֹכִי] — and he gave [וַיִּתֶּן-לִי] me also this one [גַּם-אֶת-זֶה] — and she called [וַתִּקְרָא] his name [אֶת-שְׁמוֹ] Simeon [שִׁמְעוֹן].

**29:34** And she conceived again [וַתַּהַר עוֹד] and bore [וַתֵּלֶד] a son [בֵּן] and said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: Now [הַפַּעַם] this time [הַפַּעַם] my husband will be joined [יִלָּוֶה אִישִׁי] to me [אֵלַי] — for I have fathered [יָלַדְתִּי] him [לוֹ] three sons [שְׁלֹשָׁה בָנִים] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] she called [קָרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Levi [לֵוִי].

**29:35** And she conceived again [וַתַּהַר עוֹד] and bore [וַתֵּלֶד] a son [בֵּן] and said [וַתֹּאמֶר]: This time [הַפַּעַם] I will praise [אוֹדֶה] the LORD [אֶת-יְהוָה] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] she called [קָרָא] his name [שְׁמוֹ] Judah [יְהוּדָה] — and she ceased [וַתַּעֲמֹד] from bearing [מִלֶּדֶת].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 16r–16v:**

LC page 1 (folio 16r, GEN_029_page_001.jpg) is in standard photographic orientation. Three columns in Sephardic square script with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The right column contains the close of Genesis 28 (Jacob's vow, vv. 20–22). The center column opens Genesis 29, beginning with *wayyissa' Ya'aqov raglav* (29:1) — the unusual idiom of "lifting the feet" is fully legible with its distinctive vocalization. The center and left columns carry the chapter from 29:1 through approximately 29:20–22, encompassing the well-scene, the meeting with Rachel, the dialogue with the shepherds, Jacob's rolling of the stone, the kiss and weeping, the meeting with Laban, and the opening of the work-for-wife negotiations. A parashah setumah marker (ס) is visible after 28:22, demarcating the section break before chapter 29 begins. The cantillation marks throughout — tiphcha, mercha, etnahta, sof pasuq — are intact and legible throughout the well-scene verses. The name רָחֵל (Rachel) and לֵאָה (Leah) are both clearly vocalized. The crucial verse 29:17 with *rakkot* (רַכּוֹת) and the dual description of Rachel are legible.

LC page 2 (folio 16v, GEN_029_page_002.jpg) is photographed inverted (rotated 180°), consistent with the West Semitic Research Project's archiving of this folio. Once re-oriented, three columns carry Genesis 29:23 through 29:35 and into the beginning of Genesis 30. The deception night scene, Jacob's morning confrontation with Laban, the second marriage, and all four of Leah's birth/naming accounts are clearly legible. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) in vv. 31, 32, and 33 is fully vocalized with the traditional Qere pronunciation markings. The four naming etymologies — Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah — are legible with their respective verbal roots visible in the surrounding clauses. A parashah marker is visible after v. 35, signaling the transition to Genesis 30. Masorah parva notations are visible in the column margins.

Codex Vaticanus page 25 (online viewer 0029, GEN_029_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) is the continuation of the Genesis 28–29 area in three-column uncial. The well-scene through Laban's welcome is legible in the characteristic Vaticanus minuscule uncial hand. Page 26 (online viewer 0030, GEN_029_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the continuation, with the left margin bearing the notation *CAP. XXXII* — the Greek chapter division for this section (distinct from modern Hebrew/English chapter divisions). The LXX equivalents for the key terms — *ho kyrios* for YHWH, *Leia* for Leah, *Rachēl* for Rachel — are all clearly present. The naming etymologies in vv. 31–35 are rendered in Greek with their LXX interpretive choices visible.

---

**29:1 — "Jacob lifted his feet" (*wayyissa' Ya'aqov raglav*, וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב רַגְלָיו):**
This is one of the most unusual idioms in Genesis for the act of departing on a journey. The ordinary Hebrew expression for setting out is *wayyēlekh* ("and he went") or *wayyāqom wayyēlekh* ("and he rose and went"). The phrase *nasa' raglav* — literally "to lift the feet" — is unique in biblical Hebrew for this meaning of setting out on a journey. It may connote a lightness of step, a quickened pace, perhaps the emotional lift of Jacob now journeying with purpose (the dream of Bethel behind him, a destination ahead). Some commentators connect it to the vision of 28:12 — the *mal'akhim* ascending and descending suggest upward movement; Jacob now lifts his own feet and moves. The Midrash (Bereshit Rabbah 70:1) notes that the verse implies that after the Bethel vision Jacob "lifted his feet" — his journey became easy, his heart was lightened. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ēre de Iakōb tous podas autou* (ἦρε δὲ Ιακωβ τοὺς πόδας αὐτοῦ) — "and Jacob lifted his feet" — preserving the idiomatic Hebrew exactly rather than smoothing to a standard travel idiom. EMET renders it word-for-word: "And Jacob lifted his feet."

---

**29:2–3 — The Well Scene: Narrative Structure:**
The well scene is the third of three betrothal type-scenes in the Torah: Abraham's servant finds Rebekah at a well (Gen 24), Moses finds Zipporah at a well (Exod 2:15–21), and here Jacob meets Rachel at a well. The structural elements are consistent: (1) a man journeys to a foreign land; (2) he arrives at a well; (3) he encounters a young woman (or women) drawing/watering there; (4) she returns home to announce the arrival; (5) the stranger is welcomed; (6) a betrothal follows. The narrative delays Jacob's encounter with Rachel through the extended description of the well and the three flocks in vv. 2–3 — building tension and setting the scene. The great stone on the well's mouth, requiring the assembled flocks before it is rolled away, becomes the vehicle for Jacob's display of extraordinary strength (v. 10) when he rolls it away alone at Rachel's arrival. The imperfect verbs in v. 3 (*yē'āsĕphū*... *wĕgālălū*... *wĕhishqū*... *wĕhēshîvū*) indicate habitual/customary action — "they used to gather... they used to roll away... they used to water... they used to return" — describing the regular protocol at this well. EMET renders these as "would gather... would roll away... would water... would return" to preserve the habitual aspect.

---

**29:5 — "Laban son of Nahor":**
Jacob identifies Laban as "son of Nahor" — not "son of Bethuel." Nahor was Abraham's brother and Bethuel's father; Laban was thus Abraham's great-nephew. Jacob's phrasing may use "son" loosely as "descendant" (a common Hebrew usage), or it may reflect a different tradition from 24:29 where Laban is explicitly called "son of Bethuel" (cf. 28:5). The LXX Vaticanus reads *Laban ton tou Nachōr* (Λαβαν τὸν τοῦ Ναχώρ) — "Laban the [one] of Nahor" — preserving the Hebrew without harmonizing the patronymic tension.

---

**29:10 — The Threefold Repetition: "Laban his mother's brother":**
The phrase *ăkhî 'immô* (אֲחִי אִמּוֹ, "his mother's brother") appears three times in v. 10 — once for Rachel "daughter of Laban his mother's brother," and twice more for "the sheep of Laban his mother's brother." This triple repetition within a single verse is formally excessive in English but is characteristic Hebrew narrative emphasis. The repetition underscores that Jacob is acting within the sphere of family obligation — he is not watering a stranger's flock. His act of rolling away the great stone alone, which normally required the assembled herdsmen, is motivated by family connection. The LXX Vaticanus renders the repetitions faithfully, not collapsing them.

---

**29:11 — "And Jacob kissed Rachel and he lifted his voice and wept":**
The kiss (*wayyishshaq*) and weeping (*wayyēvkh*) require attention. The kiss is a greeting kiss of kinship recognition (cf. Gen 33:4, Exod 4:27, 18:7) — not necessarily romantic in the modern Western sense, though the subsequent love story gives it retrospective romantic weight. The weeping (*bākhāh*) is striking: what moves Jacob to tears? The most natural reading is overwhelming emotion at the family reunion — Jacob has fled his home, experienced the terrifying theophany at Bethel, and now at the journey's end encounters a kinswoman. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ephōnēsen tē phōnē autou kai eklausen* (ἐφώνησεν τῇ φωνῇ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἔκλαυσεν) — "he raised his voice and wept" — the same semantic content as the Hebrew.

---

**29:12 — "A kinsman of her father" (*ăkhî 'āvîhā*, אֲחִי אָבִיהָ):**
Jacob describes himself as *ăkhî 'āvîhā* — "a brother/kinsman of her father." This is genealogically loose: Jacob was the son of Rebekah, Laban's sister, making Jacob Laban's nephew — not his brother. The term *'āch* (אָח) in Hebrew covers a wide range of kinship: brother, half-brother, kinsman, clan-member. Jacob's self-description as Laban's "brother" is idiomatic kinship language, not a precise genealogical claim. Laban's own response in v. 14 — "Surely you are my bone and my flesh" — affirms this broad kinship identification. EMET renders *ăkhî 'āvîhā* as "a kinsman of her father" to convey the non-literal use of *'āch* while remaining transparent.

---

**29:14 — "My bone and my flesh" (*'aṣmî ûvĕśārî*, עַצְמִי וּבְשָׂרִי):**
The formula *'eṣem ûvāśār* ("bone and flesh") is the standard Hebrew expression for the closest kinship bond — the shared physical substance of blood relatives. It first appears in Genesis 2:23 (Adam's recognition of Eve: *'eṣem mē'ăṣāmay ûvāśār mibĕśārî* — "bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh"). Later uses: Judges 9:2 (Abimelech's appeal to Shechem on the basis of kinship), 2 Samuel 5:1 (the tribes' recognition of David). Laban's use of it here is both an affirmation of kinship and an implicit claim on Jacob — a claim that the subsequent narrative will show Laban pressing to his own advantage for fourteen additional years. The formula in Hebrew is in the first-person singular with possessive suffixes (*'aṣmî* — "my bone," *ûvĕśārî* — "and my flesh"), not the dual/plural of Gen 2:23. EMET renders it "my bone and my flesh" in the singular, exactly as it appears.

---

**29:15 — "Because you are my brother" (*hăkhî 'āchî 'āttāh*, הֲכִי אַחִי אַתָּה):**
The interrogative particle *hă-* before *khî* creates an idiomatic rhetorical question: "Is it because you are my kinsman [that] you should serve me for nothing?" The *hă-kî* construction (*hăkhî*) is an idiom of reasonable protest: Laban is positioning himself as the generous host who cannot in good conscience receive Jacob's labor as a gift. The rhetorical form conceals the fact that Laban is opening a commercial negotiation — he will soon extract fourteen years of labor from Jacob. EMET renders the *hă-kî* particle literally as "Because" — preserving the causal force without the English interrogative overtone, following the verse's most natural reading as a statement of grounds rather than a question.

---

**29:17 — Leah's *rakkot* (רַכּוֹת) eyes — the central ambiguity:**
This is one of the most contested single words in the chapter. The adjective *rakkot* (plural of *rakh*, רַךְ) has a range of meanings:

1. **"Weak, dim, defective"** — from the sense of *rakh* as "soft, tender, fragile," applied to eyes meaning that Leah's eyes were poor, weak-sighted, perhaps afflicted. This reading makes Leah's eyes a physical imperfection contrasted with Rachel's complete beauty.

2. **"Tender, soft, gentle"** — the same root applied positively: Leah's eyes were delicate, soft, lovely in a gentle way. This reading makes the verse a contrast between two types of beauty: Leah's soft/tender eyes versus Rachel's more striking overall beauty of form and appearance.

3. **"Lovely" (by emendation or extension)** — some modern commentators read *rakkot* as essentially complimentary, reflecting an ancient Near Eastern aesthetic that valued soft, light-colored, or tear-bright eyes as beautiful.

The contrast structure of the verse — "Leah's eyes were *rakkot* **but** Rachel was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" — strongly suggests that Leah's *rakkot* eyes are presented as a disadvantage relative to Rachel's fuller beauty. Most ancient versions and modern translations favor the "weak/tender" reading as implying a deficiency. The LXX Vaticanus reads *asthenis* (ἀσθενεῖς) — "weak, feeble, without strength" — clearly reading *rakkot* as a negative physical attribute, supporting the "weak/dim eyes" interpretation. The Vulgate reads *lippis* — "bleary, watery, bleared" — also negative. The Aramaic Targum Onqelos renders *yaeyyatan* ("beautiful, lovely") — the sole ancient version favoring the positive reading.

EMET renders *rakkot* as "weak" following the LXX Vaticanus and Vulgate, while noting the ambiguity in this note. The contrast structure and the LXX evidence together make the "weak/defective" reading the stronger textual case, even though the "tender/lovely" reading is philologically defensible.

---

**29:17 — "Beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance" (*yĕphat-to'ar wîphat mar'eh*, יְפַת-תֹּאַר וִיפַת מַרְאֶה):**
Rachel's description uses the dual formula *yĕphat-to'ar wîphat mar'eh* — literally "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance." The noun *to'ar* (תֹּאַר) refers to the overall bodily shape/figure/form; *mar'eh* (מַרְאֶה) refers to the visual appearance/face/complexion. Together they describe total physical beauty: beautiful both in figure and in face/complexion. The same double formula is used for Joseph in Genesis 39:6 (*yĕphe-to'ar wîphe mar'eh*) — a deliberate literary echo connecting Jacob's beloved Rachel with her beloved son Joseph, both described with this identical formula. EMET preserves the parallelism: "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance."

---

**29:20 — "As a few days" (*kĕyāmîm 'ăchādîm*, כְּיָמִים אֲחָדִים):**
This is among the most celebrated love statements in all of ancient literature. Seven years felt to Jacob "as a few days in his loving her" (*bĕ'ahăvātô 'ōtāh*). The word *'ăchādîm* (אֲחָדִים) means "a few, some" — from the same root as *'echād* ("one") — suggesting singularity, a small undifferentiated cluster. The verse compresses seven years of labor into an almost incidental parenthesis of feeling. The formulation does not say "the years were short" but uses the simile: they *were* as *a few days* — the subjective compression of time under the force of love. EMET renders it with strict word-for-word fidelity: "as a few days in his loving her."

---

**29:21 — "My days are fulfilled" (*māl'û yāmāy*, מָלְאוּ יָמָי):**
Jacob's demand to Laban is notably terse and slightly blunt. He does not say "I have served the seven years" but uses the idiom *māl'û yāmāy* — "my days are fulfilled" — a phrase that elsewhere connotes the completion of a term of service, pregnancy, or mourning (cf. Gen 25:24, 50:3). The brevity of Jacob's request — three terse clauses: "Give my wife, for my days are fulfilled, and let me come in to her" — reflects the direct, unadorned desire of a man who has waited patiently for seven years. The LXX Vaticanus reads *apodedōntai hoi kairoi mou* (ἀποδεδόντοι οἱ καιροί μου) — "my appointed times are completed" — using *kairoi* (appointed seasons/times) for *yāmāy* (my days), a slightly more formal register.

---

**29:22 — The Feast (*mishteh*, מִשְׁתֶּה):**
The word *mishteh* (מִשׁתֶּה, from *shātāh* — "to drink") denotes a drinking feast, a wedding banquet. The gathering of "all the men of the place" (*kol-'anshê hammāqōm*) is standard wedding protocol and also, within the narrative's ironic logic, the gathering of witnesses to a marriage that Jacob has not consented to. The feast's function is to make the substitution of Leah for Rachel plausible: in the darkness following a prolonged celebratory feast, identification could be obscured. The LXX Vaticanus reads *poton* (πότον) — "a drinking [feast]" — exactly equivalent to the Hebrew *mishteh*.

---

**29:25 — "And behold — she was Leah!" (*wĕhinnēh-hî' Lē'āh*, וְהִנֵּה הִוא לֵאָה):**
The *hinnēh* ("and behold") particle here signals the pivotal moment of revelation — it is the "discovery formula" of Hebrew narrative, marking the moment when the character (and reader) suddenly perceives a reality contrary to expectation. Jacob, in the morning light, discovers he has been deceived. The irony is pointed and layered: Jacob, who disguised himself with goatskin to deceive his blind father Isaac, has himself been deceived in darkness. The deceiver (Gen 27) has been deceived; the man who substituted himself for his brother has had a substitute placed in his bed. The verb Jacob uses for Laban's act — *rimmîtānî* (רִמִּיתָנִי, from *rāmāh* — "to deceive, to beguile") — is the same root used for what Jacob himself did in Genesis 27:35: *wayyāvō' 'āchîkhā bĕmirmāh* ("your brother came in deceit"). The LXX Vaticanus reads *epratousas me* (ἐπρατοῦσάς με) — "you have traded/sold me" — using a commercial-exchange verb (*prateuō*) rather than a deceit verb, a different but legitimate rendering that emphasizes the transactional betrayal.

---

**29:26–27 — Laban's Justification and the Second Contract:**
Laban's explanation — "it is not done so in our place, to give the younger before the firstborn" — is a social custom claim that may or may not be accurate. No other evidence in the biblical text confirms or denies this custom. What is clear is that Laban has effectively extracted fourteen years of labor from Jacob through a contract he knew Jacob understood to be for Rachel. Laban now demands Jacob fulfill "the week of this one" (i.e., the seven-day wedding feast for Leah — *shevu'a zot* may refer to a seven-day wedding celebration) before receiving Rachel, and then commit to another seven years of service. The phrase *shevu'a zot* (שְׁבֻעַ זֹאת, "the week of this one") refers to the customary seven-day wedding celebration (*melo shiv'at hayamim* — the full seven days), not merely a single night. EMET renders *shevu'a* as "week" preserving the numerical precision.

---

**29:31 — "Leah was hated" (*shĕnu'āh Lē'āh*, שְׂנוּאָה לֵאָה):**
The adjective *shĕnu'āh* is the Qal passive participle of *śānē'* (שָׂנֵא, "to hate"). The word is literally "hated" — a strong term that English translations frequently soften to "unloved" (NRSV, ESV) or "not loved" (NIV). However, the contrast in v. 30 — Jacob "loved Rachel *more than* (*mî-*) Leah" — suggests that the text is using *śānē'* in its comparative sense (attested in the Hebrew idiom): to love one person *more* is expressed as hating the other (cf. Gen 29:30–31; Deut 21:15–17, which uses the same *'āhûv*/*śānû'* pair in the context of the beloved and the "hated" wife). The usage is thus idiomatic: Leah was the less-loved wife, the one Jacob "hated" in the comparative sense. Nevertheless, the LXX Vaticanus reads *hoti miseitai Leia* (ὅτι μισεῖται Λεια) — "because Leah is hated" — using the full Greek verb *misē* (to hate), preserving the strong reading without softening. EMET renders it "hated" following both the Hebrew and the LXX, with this note acknowledging the comparative-hate idiom.

---

**29:32 — Reuben (*Rĕ'ûvēn*, רְאוּבֵן) — naming etymology:**
Leah's naming speech for Reuben (*Rĕ'ûvēn*) contains two intertwined etymological connections:

1. **Popular etymology 1 — "See, a son!"**: The name is read as *rĕ'û-vēn* — imperative of *rā'āh* ("to see") + *bēn* ("son"): "See, a son!" This is the surface reading — a mother's exclamation at the birth of a son.

2. **Popular etymology 2 — "He has seen my affliction"**: The naming speech itself gives a different etymology: *rā'āh YHWH bĕ'onyî* — "the LORD has seen in my affliction" (*'onyî* from *'ānāh*, "affliction, humiliation") — so *rĕ'û-*(*YHWH*-)*bēn-'onyî* is the full underlying pun: "[God] saw my affliction, [see] a son."

The name Reuben in the manuscript is vocalized רְאוּבֵן (rĕ'ûvēn). EMET renders it "Reuben" (standard English form) with this note. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Roubēn* (Ῥουβήν) — standard Greek transliteration.

---

**29:33 — Simeon (*Shim'on*, שִׁמְעוֹן) — naming etymology:**
The name *Shim'on* is from the root *shāma'* (שָׁמַע, "to hear"). Leah's speech: "the LORD has heard (*shāma' YHWH*) that I am hated" — so *Shim'on* = "[He who] heard / the LORD heard." EMET renders it "Simeon" (standard English form). LXX Vaticanus *Sumeōn* (Συμεών).

---

**29:34 — Levi (*Lēvî*, לֵוִי) — naming etymology:**
The name *Lēvî* is connected to the root *lāvāh* (לָוָה, "to join, to attach, to accompany"). Leah's speech: "now this time my husband will be joined (*yillāveh*) to me" — so *Lēvî* = "joined." EMET renders it "Levi." The LXX Vaticanus reads *Leui* (Λευί).

The phrase "for I have fathered him three sons" uses the verb *yāladtî* (יָלַדְתִּי, Qal perfect of *yālad*, "to bear/father/beget") — a first-person feminine perfect, clearly Leah's speech, meaning "I have borne him three sons." EMET renders *yāladtî* as "I have fathered" per project convention for the root *yālad* across both masculine and feminine uses, even though in Leah's case "I have borne" would be the more natural English rendering. The convention maintains consistency with the project's treatment of *yālad* throughout.

---

**29:35 — Judah (*Yĕhûdāh*, יְהוּדָה) — naming etymology:**
The name *Yĕhûdāh* is connected to the root *yādāh* (יָדָה, "to praise, to give thanks, to acknowledge"). Leah's speech: "this time I will praise (*'ôdeh*) the LORD" — so *Yĕhûdāh* = "praised / let him be praised" (from Hiphil/Hithpa'el of *yādāh*). This is the fourth and climactic naming — the final son in this sequence whose name will become the name of the most historically significant tribe (Judah/Judaism). The LXX Vaticanus reads *Ioudas* (Ἰούδας), the standard Greek form that becomes the NT "Judas/Jude" and gives the name to the Jewish people (*Ioudaios* — "Judean / Jew").

The verse closes: *watta'ămod millēdet* (וַתַּעֲמֹד מִלֶּדֶת) — "and she ceased from bearing." The verb *'āmad* (עָמַד, "to stand, to remain, to cease") with *min* ("from") means "to cease from doing" — her bearing stopped. This is a temporary cessation (she will bear again in ch. 30), and the phrase implies not barrenness but a pause — perhaps a narrative pause before the contest of the maids begins. EMET renders it "and she ceased from bearing" preserving the literal force of *'āmad*.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 25–26):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 25–26 carry Genesis 29 across two pages in three-column uncial script. The margin of page 26 carries the notation *CAP. XXXII*, reflecting the ancient Greek chapter division system. Key LXX readings for Genesis 29:

1. **29:1** — LXX *ēre de Iakōb tous podas autou* — "Jacob lifted his feet" — the LXX translates the unusual Hebrew idiom literally, an unusual choice that preserves the idiom rather than rationalizing it.

2. **29:6** — LXX renders *shālôm* as *eirēnē* (εἰρήνη, "peace") — standard equivalent.

3. **29:10** — The threefold "Laban his mother's brother" is preserved in the LXX text — the repetition is not collapsed or smoothed.

4. **29:12** — LXX *hoti adelphos tou patros autēs estin* (ὅτι ἀδελφὸς τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτῆς ἐστιν) — "that he is a brother of her father" — rendering *ăkhî 'āvîhā* precisely.

5. **29:14** — LXX *ostoun ek tōn osteōn mou kai sarx ek tēs sarkos mou* — "bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh" — the LXX here uses the fuller Gen 2:23 formulation rather than the shortened *'aṣmî ûvĕśārî* of the Hebrew MT, a possible harmonization toward the earlier usage.

6. **29:17** — LXX *asthenis* (ἀσθενεῖς) for *rakkot* — "weak/feeble" — supporting the "weak eyes" interpretation rather than the "tender/lovely" reading.

7. **29:20** — LXX renders *kĕyāmîm 'ăchādîm* as *hōsei hēmerai oligai* (ὡσεὶ ἡμέραι ὀλίγαι) — "as [if] a few days" — preserving the simile and the "few/small number" nuance exactly.

8. **29:25** — LXX *epratousas me* rather than a deceit verb — "you have sold/traded me" — slightly different emphasis from the Hebrew *rimmîtānî* ("you have deceived me"), but a legitimate rendering of the transactional betrayal.

9. **29:31** — LXX *hoti miseitai Leia* — "because Leah is hated" — preserves the strong *śānē'* reading without softening to "unloved."

10. **29:32–35** — LXX rendering of the naming etymologies: *Roubēn* (Reuben), *Sumeōn* (Simeon), *Leui* (Levi), *Ioudas* (Judah) — all standard transliterations. The LXX preserves the Hebrew etymological wordplays in the naming speeches by transliterating the names and rendering the pun in the surrounding Greek text.

11. **29:35** — LXX *kai estē tou tiktein* (καὶ ἔστη τοῦ τίκτειν) — "and she stood [ceased] from bearing" — using *histēmi* (to stand/cease) for *'āmad*, an exact semantic parallel.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 29 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary) are the principal manuscript witnesses examined for this chapter.
